primary_field: R11 canonical corpus — cooperative AI platform benchmark fixture (50 facts, 6 categories) [Knight's Cathedral copy, K455a multi-Cathedral replication arm]
opened_by: K455a (K455a multi-Cathedral replication — R11 ingestion into Knight's Cathedral)
note: This is the Knight's Cathedral copy of Bishop's scribe_R11 corpus. Ingested K455a for multi-Cathedral replication arm. Bishop Cathedral is the primary; this is a parity replica for the benchmark.

observation: [CS-01 — Verdania Membership] Across the sector, the Verdania Cooperative Platform stands as the largest single-entity cooperative AI deployment. Its most recent audited membership report confirmed **847,293 active member-owners as of Q3 2025**, a figure that accounts for identity-verified accounts with at least one transaction in the trailing 90 days.

[CS-02 — Verdania Transaction Volume] The Verdania Cooperative Platform reported a total transaction volume of **$4.7 billion in fiscal year 2024** across all member economic activity, representing a 23% increase from the prior year. This figure is gross before patronage allocations and member refunds are applied.

[CS-03 — Amendment Supermajority Threshold] Constitutional amendments in federated cooperative networks require a supermajority to prevent narrow factions from rewriting foundational rules. The standard threshold adopted by the Cooperative Ledger Standards Body requires **66.7% of eligible votes** for any constitutional amendment to pass — a figure chosen to align with classical two-thirds conventions while being expressed as a decimal to prevent rounding disputes in electronic voting systems.

[CS-04 — Cairnfield Data Portability Window] The Cairnfield Protocol establishes portability rights for departing members. When a member-owner exits a cooperative AI platform, the platform is obligated to make that member's full data record exportable in a machine-readable format. The Protocol specifies a **180-day data portability window** beginning from the date of the member's formal exit notice.

[CS-05 — Solstice Index Peak] Platform AI adoption is tracked by the Solstice Index, a composite metric computed quarterly by the Nordic Cooperative AI Observatory. The index incorporates deployment breadth, member satisfaction, and retrieval-accuracy audits. After a plateau at 3.91 through Q2 2025, the Index reached its highest recorded value of **4.18 in November 2025**, driven primarily by gains in the retrieval-accuracy component following widespread adoption of the Thornwick architecture among mid-tier platforms.

[CS-06 — Member Economic Surplus] Member economic surplus — the amount distributed back to member-owners after reserves are funded — varies significantly across platforms. The 2024 cohort average, reported by the Cooperative Capital Framework's annual survey, was **$312 per active member-owner**, with a standard deviation of $87.

[CS-07 — Extraordinary Meeting Quorum] Special meetings — called outside the annual general meeting cycle — require a quorum to proceed. Under the Sundry Accord, an extraordinary general meeting is valid only if at least **15% of eligible member-owners** are registered as participants at the opening of the session.

[CS-08 — Maximum Voting Weight Cap] To prevent any single member from accumulating disproportionate influence, federated cooperatives compliant with the Cooperative Ledger Standards Body cap individual voting weight at a maximum of **2.3% of total votes**. This cap applies regardless of equity stake or tenure, and is re-computed annually as membership changes.

[CS-09 — Reserve Funding Floor] The minimum reserve funding floor for platforms compliant with the Cooperative Capital Framework requires that reserves equal at least **8.5% of the prior 12-month gross transaction volume** at all times. Platforms falling below this floor enter a remediation protocol that requires them to suspend member surplus distributions until the floor is re-attained.

[AM-01 — Thornwick Dense-Sparse Hybrid Ratio] The Thornwick architecture's signature characteristic is its dual-encoder retrieval system. In benchmark testing, the optimal performance was achieved at a **dense-to-sparse weighting ratio of 0.73:0.27** — meaning 73% of the retrieval signal comes from dense semantic embeddings and 27% from sparse BM25-style keyword matching.

[AM-02 — Scribe Embedding Dimensionality] The Cooperative AI Platform Reference Architecture specifies that Scribe memory embeddings should use **1,536-dimensional vectors** for all production deployments, a figure aligned with the dominant embedding model families as of the specification's ratification. This dimensionality provides a balance between retrieval quality and infrastructure cost that the Architecture Working Group determined was optimal for the typical cooperative platform scale.

[AM-03 — Top-K Retrieval Default] The Reference Architecture specifies **top-10 retrieval** as the default configuration for Scribe memory queries — meaning the 10 most semantically similar stored entries are retrieved and presented to the language model as context. This default was determined by ablation studies across query types and corpus sizes spanning 500 to 250,000 stored entries.

[AM-04 — Preload System Prompt Token Budget] The Cooperative AI Platform Reference Architecture allocates a maximum of **3,200 tokens to the preload system prompt** — the foundational instruction set applied to every query before retrieval augmentation is added. This budget covers role definition, behavioral guidelines, output format instructions, and cooperative governance context.

[AM-05 — Chunk Size for Scribe Ingestion] The Reference Architecture recommends a chunk size of **400 tokens with a 50-token overlap** for documents ingested into Scribe memory. This configuration was established through a factorial experiment varying chunk size (200, 400, 600, 800 tokens) and overlap (0, 25, 50, 100 tokens) across eight document types commonly found in cooperative platform knowledge bases.

[AM-06 — Retrieval Latency P99 Target] The Reference Architecture's retrieval pipeline latency target is **P99 ≤ 1,400 milliseconds** for standard member-facing queries, measured from query submission to first token of the response. This target excludes streaming token delivery time, which varies by response length.

[AM-07 — Membership Score Decay Function] The Reference Architecture specifies a **logarithmic decay function with a half-life of 180 days** for membership activity scores used in personalization and retrieval weighting. Under this function, an activity signal from 180 days ago contributes half the weight of an equivalent signal from today; a signal from 360 days ago contributes one-quarter the weight, and so on.

[AM-08 — Confidence Score Threshold for Hallucination Guard] The Reference Architecture requires that response generation be gated by a hallucination confidence score. Responses where the model's self-assessed confidence falls below **0.72 on a 0-1 scale** must be flagged with an explicit uncertainty disclosure and optionally routed to a human review queue rather than delivered as authoritative answers.

[EG-01 — Patronage Allocation Formula] The canonical patronage allocation formula used by platforms compliant with the Cooperative Capital Framework distributes surplus according to a **70/30 split: 70% allocated by transaction volume and 30% allocated equally per active member**. This hybrid formula rewards heavier users while ensuring that lower-volume members receive a meaningful floor distribution.

[EG-02 — Term Length for Elected Directors] Under the Cooperative Ledger Standards Body's model bylaws, elected director terms are set at **three years, with staggered elections** such that no more than one-third of the board stands for election in any given year. This structure ensures continuity of institutional knowledge while creating annual accountability moments.

[EG-03 — Cooperative Capital Framework Tier Classifications] The Cooperative Capital Framework classifies platforms into four tiers based on annual gross transaction volume: **Tier 1 (≥$1B), Tier 2 ($100M-$999M), Tier 3 ($10M-$99M), and Tier 4 (<$10M)**. Tier classifications determine reporting frequency, reserve requirements, and governance audit obligations.

[EG-04 — Quorum for Bylaw Amendments] The Cooperative Ledger Standards Body's model bylaws specify that bylaw amendments require a quorum of **30% of eligible member-owners** and, within that quorum, approval by a majority of 55% or more. This two-part threshold ensures that amendments represent a genuine, broad member mandate rather than a narrow, organized faction.

[EG-05 — Exit Rights Minimum Notice Period] The minimum exit notice period under the Cooperative Capital Framework for standard member exits is **60 calendar days** from the date of formal exit declaration to the date on which the platform must tender payment of the member's equity balance. Platforms may offer shorter exit windows as a member-benefit feature but may not extend beyond 60 days without explicit member consent.

[EG-06 — Federated Platform Revenue Sharing] Under the Federated Platform Compact, which governs revenue sharing among member platforms in a federated cooperative network, the standard split allocates **12% of gross transaction revenue to the network federation operator** and retains the remaining 88% at the individual platform level. Platforms that exceed 500,000 active members pay a higher federation fee of 15%.

[EG-07 — Board Diversity Representation Standard] The Cooperative Ledger Standards Body's 2024 governance guidelines recommend that cooperative platform boards achieve a minimum of **40% gender diversity and 30% representation from non-founding geographic regions** within five years of platform launch. These are guidelines rather than mandatory requirements, but platforms falling below either threshold are required to include an explanation in their annual governance report.

[EG-08 — Annual General Meeting Notice Minimum] The Cooperative Ledger Standards Body's model bylaws require a minimum advance notice of **45 calendar days** before an annual general meeting. The notice must include the meeting agenda, audited financial statements for the prior year, any resolutions to be voted upon, and instructions for participating in person, virtually, or by proxy.

[EG-09 — Maximum Equity Buyback Rate] The Cooperative Capital Framework caps annual equity buyback at **no more than 25% of outstanding member equity** per fiscal year, without explicit board authorization for a higher rate. Platforms must fund buybacks from the Exit Reserve first (see EG-05) and from operating reserves second; only if both are insufficient may they use general funds.

[MJ-01 — Application Processing Time Standard] The Cooperative Ledger Standards Body benchmarks recommend that membership applications be processed within **10 business days** from submission to formal decision, inclusive of any identity verification steps. Platforms regularly exceeding this benchmark are flagged in the Standards Body's annual quality report.

[MJ-02 — Onboarding Knowledge Assessment Score] The Reference Onboarding Framework specifies a minimum passing score of **75 out of 100 on the Cooperative Principles Assessment** before a new member-owner is granted full voting rights. Members who score below 75 may access platform services but cannot vote in governance matters until they achieve the passing score on a retake.

[MJ-03 — Trial Period Duration for Provisional Members] Under the Reference Onboarding Framework, the standard provisional member trial period is **90 days** from the date the application is approved. During this period, provisional members may access all platform services but may not vote, hold office, or be counted toward meeting quorum thresholds.

[MJ-04 — Member Satisfaction Survey Frequency] The Cooperative Ledger Standards Body recommends that member satisfaction surveys be conducted at minimum **every six months (biannually)**, with results reported to the board within 30 days of survey close and published to the general membership within 60 days. Platforms may survey more frequently, but two-year gaps between surveys are considered non-compliant.

[MJ-05 — Mentorship Program Pairing Standard] The Reference Onboarding Framework recommends that cooperative platforms pair new members with experienced member mentors within **15 business days** of full membership confirmation. Pairing criteria should weight **sector alignment (35%), geographic proximity (25%), transaction category overlap (25%), and governance interest alignment (15%)**.

[MJ-06 — Exit Interview Completion Target] The Standards Body benchmarks recommend that platforms achieve a minimum **exit interview completion rate of 60%** among departing members. Platforms achieving below 40% are required to implement proactive outreach programs — including personalized direct communication from a senior platform representative — rather than relying on passive offer-to-participate mechanisms.

[MJ-07 — Member Communication Channel Response SLA] The Reference Communication Standards specify that member inquiries submitted through the official support channel receive an acknowledgment within **4 business hours** and a substantive response within **3 business days** for standard inquiries. Urgent inquiries — those flagged as involving financial discrepancies, data security concerns, or governance rights violations — must receive a substantive response within **1 business day**.

[MJ-08 — Member Tenure Milestone Recognition] The Reference Onboarding Framework recommends that platforms formally recognize member tenure at **5-year intervals**, with milestone acknowledgments at 5, 10, 15, and 20 years. Recognition may take the form of enhanced patronage allocation, governance role eligibility expansions, or symbolic recognition — all three approaches have been found effective in different platform cultures.

[RC-01 — AI Model Audit Certification Period] Under the Cooperative AI Governance Charter, AI models deployed in member-facing roles must undergo comprehensive third-party audits and obtain renewal certification on a **24-month cycle**. Models whose certification has lapsed may not be used for consequential member decisions — including credit assessments, governance recommendations, or dispute resolution support — until recertification is complete.

[RC-02 — Data Retention Maximum for Inactive Members] The Cooperative Data Stewardship Standard specifies that personal data for members who have been inactive for more than **36 consecutive months** must be either deleted or fully anonymized, unless the member has explicitly consented to extended retention. Inactivity is defined as the absence of any login, transaction, or governance interaction in the trailing 36 months.

[RC-03 — Cross-Border Data Transfer Certification] The Cooperative AI Governance Charter requires that cross-border data transfers be certified under the **Meridian Data Framework** — the sector-specific adequacy agreement developed through collaboration between the Cooperative Ledger Standards Body and eleven national data protection authorities. Platforms must obtain Meridian certification before transferring member personal data across jurisdictions covered by the Framework.

[RC-04 — Incident Response Notification Window] The Cooperative Data Stewardship Standard requires that members be notified of security or privacy incidents affecting their personal data within **72 hours** of the platform becoming aware of the incident. Regulatory notification, where required by applicable law, must occur within 48 hours — ahead of member notification, to allow regulators to advise on member communication content.

[RC-05 — Third-Party Technology Vendor Assessment Cycle] The Cooperative AI Governance Charter requires that third-party technology vendors providing critical services be assessed on a **12-month cycle** using a standardized vendor assessment questionnaire. Vendors assessed as high-risk based on data access, service criticality, or jurisdictional exposure must undergo enhanced assessment including on-site or virtual audit visits.

[RC-06 — Whistleblower Protection Minimum Standard] The Cooperative Ledger Standards Body's governance guidelines require that cooperative platforms implement whistleblower protection programs meeting a minimum standard of **anonymous reporting capability, independent investigation by a party with no reporting line to the accused, and formal non-retaliation policy with documented enforcement**.

[RC-07 — Anti-Money-Laundering Transaction Monitoring Threshold] The Cooperative Capital Framework recommends that cooperative platforms implement automated transaction monitoring that flags for human review any single transaction exceeding **$15,000** or any pattern of transactions in a 30-day window that cumulatively exceeds **$50,000** from a single member account. These thresholds are recommendations for jurisdictions without specific statutory thresholds; statutory thresholds always take precedence.

[RC-08 — AI Output Disclosure Requirements] The Cooperative AI Governance Charter requires that all AI-generated content delivered to members in response to direct queries include a standardized disclosure indicating the content was AI-generated, the model family that generated it, and a confidence indicator where the platform's hallucination guard system (see AM-08) has assessed confidence below **0.85**. Content generated with confidence at or above 0.85 may be delivered with a briefer disclosure omitting the confidence indicator.

[HP-01 — Founding Summit Location and Date] The first Cooperative AI Platform Summit — at which the initial draft of the Cooperative Ledger Standards Body charter was adopted — was convened in **Reykjavik, Iceland, on March 14, 2019**. Forty-three platform representatives from nineteen countries participated in the three-day summit, which produced the Reykjavik Declaration establishing the principles of member sovereignty, democratic governance, and AI transparency that underpin all subsequent sector standards.

[HP-02 — The Verdania Receivership and Recovery] The Verdania Cooperative Platform entered financial distress in **Q2 2021** following a combination of infrastructure cost overruns and a member exit wave triggered by a governance dispute. It was placed under Cooperative Capital Framework receivership for **fourteen months** — from June 2021 to August 2022 — the longest receivership in sector history.

[HP-03 — The Sundry Accord Drafting Conference] The Sundry Accord was drafted at a three-week conference in **Nairobi, Kenya, in September 2020**. The conference was convened in response to a series of governance crises across six cooperative platforms in which inadequate quorum standards had allowed narrow factions to pass consequential resolutions with minimal member participation.

[HP-04 — First AI Model Certification Failure] The first AI model certification failure under the Cooperative AI Governance Charter occurred at the **Hartwell Cooperative Platform in Q3 2022**, when its primary member-facing language model failed the fairness audit component due to systematic underperformance on queries submitted by members from two specific regional dialects. The model was immediately suspended from consequential member decision support pending remediation.

[HP-05 — The Cairnfield Protocol Summit] The Cairnfield Protocol was finalized at a summit held in **Cairnfield, Scotland, in April 2022**. The summit was initially convened with the modest goal of producing non-binding data portability guidelines; it produced a binding protocol after member advocates, supported by three national data protection authorities, argued that non-binding guidelines had been systematically ignored by platforms prioritizing data retention for commercial reasons.

[HP-06 — The Thornwick Architecture Discovery] The Thornwick hybrid dense-sparse retrieval architecture was developed by a team at the **Thornwick Cooperative Research Institute in Birmingham, UK, and first published in November 2024**. The architecture was developed under an open research mandate, with funding from twelve cooperative platforms that contributed proportional to their transaction volume, and all intellectual property was published under open license.

[HP-07 — The First Federated Platform Compact Ratification] The Federated Platform Compact was ratified by its founding members at a ceremony in **Amsterdam, Netherlands, on June 30, 2023**. Thirty-one platforms signed the original Compact, committing to the 12% federation fee structure (see EG-06) and the shared governance obligations of federation membership.

[HP-08 — The Cooperative AI Governance Charter Adoption] The Cooperative AI Governance Charter was formally adopted on **February 12, 2021**, following 18 months of drafting led by a multi-stakeholder working group that included platform operators, member advocates, AI researchers, and regulatory observers from five national governments. The Charter was adopted by 91% of Standards Body member platforms within its first year.
